frameworks.name: deep neural network connectionist model
frameworks.one_line: neural networks as a model system for human continual learning

frameworks.name: continual / sequential task learning
frameworks.one_line: learner acquires Task A, then B, then is re-tested on A

frameworks.name: transfer and interference in learning
frameworks.one_line: task similarity modulates positive transfer and retroactive interference

frameworks.name: rich vs lazy training regime
frameworks.one_line: training regime shapes representation dimensionality and individual strategy

core_question: Do humans and neural networks show similar transfer and interference patterns when learning successive rule tasks?
key_assumptions: task similarity can be parametrically varied
hidden-layer representations index how knowledge is reused
individual strategies map to representation geometry
manipulations: similarity between successive Tasks A and B
training regime in networks (rich vs lazy)
sequence order A then B then retest A
key_findings: similar successive tasks yield greater transfer to Task B and greater interference on retested Task A in both humans and networks
networks reuse existing solutions to accelerate similar tasks while corrupting prior solutions
lumpers generalise shared structure better; splitters remember unique features better
interpretations: shared structure use produces a transfer-interference trade-off in both learners
rich vs lazy training reproduces lumper/splitter individual differences via representation dimensionality
